macro_segments.title: The Debate of Resh Lakish and Rabbi Yochanan on the Verse 'Bechol Kodesh Lo Tiga'

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Dispute: What the Verse Teaches About Tamei Eating Sacrifices
Resh Lakish's Position: The Verse Teaches Both Touching and Eating
Rabbi Yochanan's Position: The Verse Refers to Terumah, Not Sacrifices
Comparison of Approaches: Resh Lakish Derives from the Verse, Rabbi Yochanan from Kareis

macro_segments.title: Tamei Eating Sacrificial Meat Before the Blood is Placed on the Altar

micro_segments.title: The Third Thing Derived from 'Bechol Kodesh': Eating Before Zerika
The Baraita: Resh Lakish Says Lashes, Rabbi Yochanan Says No Lashes
Abaye's Resolution: The Dispute Concerns Tumaat HaGuf, Not Tumaat Basar
The Extra Word 'Vehabasar' and Its Inclusion of Inedible Items Like Wood and Frankincense
Rava's Counter-Position: Tumaat Basar Excludes the Pre-Zerika Stage
Rava's Resolution: Stage Two and the Matirim Principle

macro_segments.title: Placing Limbs of a Non-Kosher Animal on the Altar: The Debate of Resh Lakish and Rabbi Yochanan

micro_segments.title: The Case: What If a Non-Kosher Animal's Limbs Are Offered on the Altar?
Resh Lakish's Position: Lashes, Based on a Negative Implicit in a Positive Command
Rabbi Yochanan's Position: No Lashes, Since the Prohibition Is Only Implied
Rabbi Yirmiyah's Challenge: The Case of Kosher Animals Proves No Lashes for Implied Prohibitions
Rav Yaakov bar Yirmiyah's Correction: The Dispute Is About Chaya, Not Behema Temeia

macro_segments.title: The Distinction Between Behema Temeia and Chaya: What Violates a Positive Command

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan: Bringing a Chaya Violates the Positive Command of Bakar VaTzon
Resh Lakish: Bringing a Chaya Is Not a Violation; It's Discretionary
Charlie's Objection: The Case of Nadav and Avihu Contradicts Resh Lakish
The Baraita's Clear Position: Only Bakar VaTzon Are Permitted; Chaya Is Invalid
Rava's Refutation of Resh Lakish: The Explicit Prohibitions in the Verses

macro_segments.title: Return to the Mishnah: Invalid Persons Catching Blood and the Status of Shirayim

micro_segments.title: The Question: Does an Invalid Kohen Catching Blood Make the Remainder Shirayim?
Rabbi Yochanan's Answer: Only Piggul Makes Shirayim; Invalid Acts Make Nothing Happen
The Logic: Piggul Requires Bracketing the Invalidity, So the Act Is Viewed as Valid

macro_segments.title: Rav Zevid's Alternative Version: The Distinction Between Invalid Person and Invalid Blood

micro_segments.title: Rav Zevid's Challenge: Why Focus on Invalid Blood Rather Than Invalid Kohen?
The Equivalence: Both Cases Should Have the Same Status Regarding Shirayim

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Question About Blood in Two Vessels: Dichuy or Shirayim?

micro_segments.title: The Case: Blood Caught in Two Vessels; One Vessel's Blood Used on the Altar
The Question: Is the Second Vessel's Blood Dichuy (Rejected) or Shirayim (Leftover)?
A Debate of Tannaim: Rabbi Eliezer Son of Rabbi Shimon vs. the Sages
The Sages' Position: Blood From Used Vessel Is Shirayim; Unused Vessels Are Dichuy
Rabbi Eliezer's Position: All Blood Is Considered Shirayim, Not Dichuy
Resolution: The Word 'Dam' (Some) Excludes Blood That Never Entered a Vessel

topical_tags.term: Tumaat HaGuf (Impurity of Body) vs. Tumaat Basar (Impurity of Meat)
Lav Haba Miklal Asei (Negative Implicit in a Positive Command)
